steps.title: Understand what needs to be supported
steps.content: Meng’s conclusion: the Ultra-Fast Robot Hand has a higher payload capacity than the Yale Model T. Payload capacity = holding force ÷ weight; higher ratio means greater capacity. The text already tells us Ultra-Fast’s holding force is more than 4× the Yale Model T’s. To support the conclusion, the graph must show that Ultra-Fast’s weight advantage over Yale is small enough that the 4× force lead is not canceled out.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘The Ultra-Fast Robot Hand and the Yale Model T each weigh more than 450 grams.’: Shows a similarity in weight, not a distinction. A shared minimum weight says nothing about which robot has the better force-to-weight ratio. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘The Ultra-Fast Robot Hand and the Yale Model T each weigh more than the Permanent Magnet Hand does.’: Again a similarity, and introduces the Permanent Magnet Hand, which is irrelevant to the Ultra-Fast vs. Yale comparison in the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘The Yale Model T has a lower holding force than the Permanent Magnet Hand despite weighing more.’: The graph shows weights, not holding forces; this claim about holding force cannot come from the graph. Also off-topic (Permanent Magnet Hand). ✗
  • Choice D — ‘The Ultra-Fast Robot Hand weighs only slightly more than the Yale Model T does.’: If Ultra-Fast has 4× the holding force but nearly the same weight, its force-to-weight ratio is far greater, directly supporting the conclusion of higher payload capacity. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
